questions.question: Which one-liner best captures the distinct job of each service?
options: SQS pulls work, SNS pushes copies, EventBridge routes by content
SQS pushes copies, SNS routes by content, EventBridge pulls work
SQS routes by content, SNS pulls work, EventBridge pushes copies
All three pull work, differing only in their consumer polling model
questions.explanation: SQS is a pull-based point-to-point work queue, SNS pushes a copy to every subscription, and EventBridge routes events to targets by matching content-based patterns. The other options scramble these roles.

questions.question: Calling `ReceiveMessage` on an SQS queue deletes the message from the queue.
questions.explanation: Receive does not delete. The message becomes invisible for the visibility timeout and reappears unless the consumer explicitly calls `DeleteMessage`; this is how SQS survives a crashing worker and why duplicates occur.

questions.question: What is the **default** SQS visibility timeout, and its maximum?
options: 30 seconds default, 12 hours maximum
30 seconds default, 14 days maximum
60 seconds default, 1 hour maximum
0 seconds default, 20 seconds maximum
questions.explanation: The visibility timeout defaults to 30 seconds and ranges from 0 seconds up to 12 hours. 14 days is the maximum message retention, and 20 seconds is the maximum long-poll wait — different limits.

questions.question: Why is long polling (`WaitTimeSeconds` up to 20) preferred over short polling for SQS?
options: It queries all servers and returns fewer empty responses, lowering cost and latency-to-first-message
It guarantees that messages are always returned in strict FIFO order
It deletes messages automatically as soon as they are received
It raises the maximum message size for the queue beyond 256 KB
questions.explanation: Long polling waits until a message arrives (or the timeout elapses) and queries all servers, so it returns fewer empty receives, cutting cost and latency. It has nothing to do with ordering, auto-delete, or message size.

questions.question: What is the default delivery guarantee across SQS Standard, SNS, and EventBridge?
options: At-least-once delivery — design every consumer to be idempotent
Exactly-once delivery — making idempotency entirely unnecessary
At-most-once delivery — messages may be dropped but never duplicated
Ordered exactly-once delivery, guaranteed end-to-end by the transport
questions.explanation: At-least-once is the default everywhere (even FIFO across its dedup boundary), so consumers must be idempotent. Exactly-once is a property built at the consumer, not handed to you by the transport.

questions.question: In an SQS FIFO queue, how does the **message group id** govern ordering and parallelism?
options: Same group id is strictly ordered, one in-flight at a time; different group ids process in parallel
All messages in the queue are globally ordered, regardless of their group id
The group id only affects deduplication and has no effect on ordering or parallelism
Each group id may have unlimited in-flight messages processed concurrently while still preserving order
questions.explanation: FIFO orders messages within a group id and keeps one message per group in-flight at a time, while different group ids run in parallel. A high-cardinality group id (e.g. per customer) preserves the ordering that matters while still scaling.

questions.question: SQS FIFO's "exactly-once" means end-to-end exactly-once delivery to the consumer, so idempotency at the consumer is no longer needed.
questions.explanation: FIFO's exactly-once is exactly-once *processing into the queue* within a 5-minute deduplication window — duplicate sends with the same dedup id inside that window are dropped. It is not end-to-end exactly-once delivery, and idempotency is still required across windows and redrives.

questions.question: Given this SQS redrive policy, what causes a message to land in the DLQ?
options: After the message has been received 5 times without being deleted, SQS moves it to the DLQ
After the message has sat in the queue for 5 days, SQS moves it to the DLQ
After 5 different consumers have subscribed to the queue, the message goes to the DLQ
After 5 seconds of visibility timeout elapse, the message is moved to the DLQ
questions.explanation: `maxReceiveCount` counts how many times a message is received without being successfully deleted; once it exceeds the limit (5 here), SQS moves the message to the DLQ, isolating poison messages. It is unrelated to retention days, subscriber count, or the visibility timeout value.

questions.question: A team plans to fan out via **SNS directly to three Lambdas**. What is the senior pushback in favor of **SNS to SQS to compute**?
options: SQS in the middle gives each consumer durability, buffering, independent retry, and a DLQ
SNS is technically unable to deliver to Lambda at all, so an SQS queue is mandatory
Lambda can never be made idempotent, whereas SQS processes every message exactly once
SNS-to-Lambda is billed at a strictly higher per-message rate than SNS-to-SQS-to-Lambda
questions.explanation: SNS-to-Lambda is deliver-and-forget with limited retry, so back-pressure is unmanaged and a down consumer can lose messages. Putting SQS in front gives each service buffering, independent retry, and a DLQ. SNS can deliver to Lambda directly, and the issue is durability, not cost or idempotency impossibility.

questions.question: For an SNS topic to fan out into an SQS queue, what permission configuration is required, and what does **raw message delivery** control?
options: The queue's resource policy must allow `sqs:SendMessage` from the topic (via `aws:SourceArn`); raw delivery off wraps the payload in an SNS envelope, on sends the bare message
The topic's resource policy must allow `sns:Subscribe` from the queue; raw delivery sets the message retention duration
No permissions are needed because SNS and SQS share a built-in trust relationship; raw delivery sets the dedup window
An IAM user access key must be embedded into the subscription; raw delivery sets the queue's visibility timeout
questions.explanation: The SQS queue's resource policy must permit `sqs:SendMessage` from the SNS topic, scoped with an `aws:SourceArn` condition. Raw message delivery off (default) wraps the payload in an SNS envelope; turning it on delivers the bare message. The other options misattribute the policy and misstate what raw delivery does.

questions.question: An event pattern must match orders of amount >= 100 from the US or Canada on nested `detail` fields. Which capability makes EventBridge the right choice over SNS filter policies?
options: EventBridge patterns match nested fields with numeric, prefix, anything-but, and exists operators that SNS cannot express as richly
EventBridge filter policies work only on top-level message attributes, exactly like SNS does
SNS filter policies natively support numeric comparison on nested JSON, so either service works equally well
Only SNS can match the `detail` block; EventBridge would require a Lambda to parse the content first
questions.explanation: EventBridge patterns match nested `detail` fields with numeric, prefix, anything-but, and exists operators — richer content-based routing than SNS filter policies, which match message attributes (or the body) more simply. The distractors invert these capabilities.

questions.question: Which capabilities does **EventBridge** provide that SNS/SQS structurally do not? (Select all that apply.)
options: Archive and replay of past events
A schema registry with typed code bindings (including .NET)
SaaS/partner event ingestion via partner event buses
Lower latency and higher raw fan-out throughput than SNS for simple broadcasts
Per-message delivery to SMS, email, and mobile push protocols
questions.explanation: EventBridge uniquely adds archive/replay, a schema registry with code bindings, and partner/SaaS ingestion. SNS, not EventBridge, wins on raw fan-out throughput/latency for simple broadcasts and natively pushes to SMS/email/mobile push protocols.

questions.question: In this idempotent handler, what happens when the same `MessageId` is delivered a second time?
options: The conditional put fails with `ConditionalCheckFailedException`, the handler returns early, and `DoWorkAsync` is skipped
The put overwrites the existing item, and `DoWorkAsync` runs again, double-processing the message
The put succeeds because DynamoDB ignores the condition on duplicates, so the work runs twice
A `ConditionalCheckFailedException` is thrown and propagates uncaught, crashing the handler
questions.explanation: The first write claims the id; on a duplicate the `attribute_not_exists(id)` condition fails, throwing `ConditionalCheckFailedException`, which is caught and triggers an early `return`, so `DoWorkAsync` never runs again. The condition prevents overwrite, and the exception is handled rather than propagated.

questions.question: Which statements about DLQ scope across the three services (and Lambda) are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: An SQS DLQ is per source queue, triggered by `maxReceiveCount`, and supports redrive to source
An SNS DLQ is per subscription, catching deliveries that fail after retries
An EventBridge DLQ is per target, catching events past retry or max event age
EventBridge uses a single account-wide DLQ shared across all rules and targets
When SNS or EventBridge invokes Lambda async, Lambda has no failure handling of its own
questions.explanation: SQS DLQs are per source queue (maxReceiveCount, redrive-to-source), SNS DLQs are per subscription, and EventBridge DLQs are per target. EventBridge does not use one account-wide DLQ, and async-invoked Lambda has its own on-failure destination — a distinct second layer.

questions.question: A Lambda has a **60s** function timeout and processes a queue whose **visibility timeout is 30s**; messages keep returning mid-processing. What is wrong and the fix?
options: The 30s visibility timeout is shorter than the 60s processing time, so messages reappear — raise it to roughly 6x the function timeout
The function timeout must always exceed the visibility timeout, so lower the function timeout to 15s instead
Long polling is disabled, which causes the redelivery — set `WaitTimeSeconds` to its maximum of 20
The queue is FIFO and is missing a message group id, so it redelivers — add a group id to fix it
questions.explanation: When the visibility timeout (30s) is shorter than worst-case processing (60s), the message becomes visible again and is redelivered before the worker finishes. The rule of thumb is to set the visibility timeout to about 6x the function timeout. Long polling and FIFO group ids do not cause this redelivery.

questions.question: You must enqueue a 5 MB document for asynchronous processing, but SQS caps messages at 256 KB. What is the correct approach?
options: Store the payload in S3 and enqueue a small pointer; the SQS Extended Client Library handles write-on-send and dereference-on-receive (up to 2 GB)
Split the document into roughly 20 separate 256 KB SQS messages and reassemble them in the consumer
Raise the SQS maximum message size limit from 256 KB to 5 MB using a queue attribute
Compress the document so it always fits under 256 KB, since the cap applies to compressed size
questions.explanation: The S3 large-payload pattern stores the body in S3 and enqueues a small pointer; the SQS Extended Client Library handles write-on-send and dereference-on-receive, supporting payloads up to 2 GB. The 256 KB cap is fixed (not raisable to 5 MB), and manual splitting or relying on compression is not the documented approach.

questions.question: Which statement about SQS delay queues and per-message message timers is correct?
options: Queue-level `DelaySeconds` hides every new message up to 15 minutes; per-message `DelaySeconds` overrides it on Standard only — FIFO has no per-message delay
Per-message `DelaySeconds` works on FIFO queues but is not supported on Standard queues
Both delay queues and per-message message timers can delay messages for up to 14 days
A delay queue retroactively hides messages already sitting in the queue, not just newly sent ones
questions.explanation: Queue-level `DelaySeconds` delays every new message up to 15 minutes; a per-message `DelaySeconds` (message timer) overrides the default but only on Standard queues — FIFO does not support per-message delay. The maximum is 15 minutes (not 14 days), and a delay queue only affects newly sent messages.

questions.question: Which AWS-to-Azure service mappings are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: SQS FIFO message-group ordering maps to Azure Service Bus sessions
SQS FIFO deduplication maps to Service Bus duplicate detection
EventBridge maps to Azure Event Grid
SQS visibility timeout maps to Azure Event Hubs partition leases
SNS filter policies map to Azure Functions triggers
questions.explanation: FIFO group-id ordering maps to Service Bus sessions, FIFO dedup to Service Bus duplicate detection, and EventBridge to Event Grid. The SQS visibility timeout maps to the Service Bus PeekLock lock duration (not Event Hubs leases), and SNS filter policies map to Service Bus subscription rules / Event Grid filters (not Functions triggers).
